返回本批次

建立产业的问题

The Problem That Built an Industry

ajitem.com 作者 ShaggyHotDog 分数 125 评论 44 2026/04/11 22:03
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 构建行业的问题 《铁芯》系列第 6 部分第 1 部分：拥有 60 年历史的基础设施，每年承载 45 亿人次。
  2. 2025 年 12 月，Technogise 的某人打开 M​​akeMyTrip 的企业平台，输入目的地，并为我预订了两趟飞往伦敦的航班。整个过程不到一分钟。一封确认电子邮件出现在我的收件箱中。出现了六个字符的预订参考号：DDTCIV 和 DHB4AL。我本来打算在 ContainerDays 2026 上发表演讲。这是一个关于容器、编排和云原生基础设施的会议：我在工作生涯中一直在思考的那种现代的、短暂的、无状态的系统。讽刺的是我是在飞机飞过来的时候才意识到的。预订这些航班的基础设施的设计可以追溯到 20 世纪 60 年代。它仍然运行在 Unix 之前的谱系上，并使用为电传打字机构建的命令语言。实现、硬件和周边软件已多次更换和升级；持续存在的是数据模型、协议和事务语义。这一切都不是在一次“大爆炸”重写中发生的：它在系统不断飞行的同时不断累积，在峰值时它仍然以每秒 10,000 笔交易的速度处理。我构建分布式系统。我以为我了解复杂的基础设施。然后我看了看自己的登机牌，拉了线。这是一个关于我的发现的六部分系列。
  3. SABRE 之前的世界要了解此基础设施存在的原因，您需要了解其构建要解决的问题。到 20 世纪 50 年代中期，美国航空公司开始通过索引卡管理预订。预订时需要打电话给工作人员，工作人员会搜索多个城市办事处的实体卡架，口头确认是否可用，然后给乘客回电话。跨大西洋预订可能需要 90 分钟才能确认。该航空公司每天在 50 多个城市处理大约 85,000 个预订请求。系统正在崩溃。 GDS（全球分销系统）的起源几乎是神话。 1953 年，在一次跨国航班上，美国航空公司总裁 C.R. 史密斯 (C.R. Smith) 坐在 IBM 推销员 R. 布莱尔·史密斯 (R. Blair Smith) 旁边。当他们着陆时，解决方案的轮廓已经勾勒出来。 IBM 和美国航空公司于 1959 年建立了正式的开发合作伙伴关系。其成果是 SABRE（半自动化商业研究环境）。它于 1964 年投入使用。从握手到签订合同用了六年，从签订合同到生产又用了五年：从对话到运行预订系统大约用了十年。大型基础设施总是按照人类的日历而不是融资平台的日历移动。同年，IBM System/360 发布。距离第一台 ATM 机问世还有三年。距离登月还有五年。第一个 VisiCalc 电子表格诞生已有 15 年。十年之内，各大航空公司纷纷效仿： GDSFounded原所有者技术基金会SABRE1964美国航空 + IBMIBM ACP / TPFApollo1971联合航空IBM TPFGalileo1987United + BA + KLM + SwissairIBM TPFWorldspan1990达美航空 + 西北 + TWAIBM TPFAmadeus1987法国航空 + 汉莎航空 + 西班牙国家航空 + SASBull 大型机 → Unix共同的线程。该表中五个源自北美的堆栈中有四个是基于 IBM TPF（或其 ACP 谱系）构建的。 Amadeus 是个例外：Bull，然后是 Unix。它们并非都落在相同的可执行运行时上，但它们必须解决相同的问题形式：共享库存和结算规则上的大量小额、延迟有限的交易，在 IATA 实践和联运经济使得分歧成本高昂的行业中。这让我想到了大多数软件工程师从未听说过的一项技术，当你读到这篇文章时，几乎可以肯定它正在处理世界某个地方的航班预订。
  4. TPF：拒绝死亡的操作系统 事务处理设施 (TPF) 是 IBM 大型机操作系统，源自 ACP（美国航空公司最初的航空公司控制程序）。它的设计目的只有一个：以亚毫秒级响应时间处理大量简单事务。它不是 Unix。它不具有 Unix 的血统、哲学或抽象概念。它比 Unix 早十年。了解 TPF 需要抛开您对现代操作系统的几乎所有了解：属性 TPF 现代操作系统进程模型没有进程。没有线程。执行和退出的短暂“程序”。进程、线程、协程内存模型每个事务的固定内存“单元”。没有堆。无动态分配。虚拟内存、堆、GCI/O 模型极快同步 I/O 到 DASD（直接访问存储）异步 I/O、块存储、NVMe 调度抢占式、基于优先级、微秒粒度通常为毫秒粒度故障模型事务级回滚。系统不崩溃；事务确实如此。取决于应用程序的主要语言汇编器。后来添加了 C。TPF 并不是您所想象的真正的操作系统。它更接近我们现在所说的事务运行时：专门构建的系统，用于接收工作单元，针对它执行一个短程序，提交状态更改，然后立即继续。面向应用程序的事务路径故意被最小化：没有长期存在的每个客户端工作线程在工作单元之间的内存中保存连接状态，并且该路径没有 Unix 风格的每个请求线程模型。 （系统级调度、I/O 和内务处理仍然存在，这并不是说机器在事务之间不执行任何操作。）这种设计是针对一个工作负载而设计的。它在该工作负载方面表现得非常出色。现代基于 TPF 的系统在正常情况下每秒处理大约 10,000 个事务。在机票销售期间，当数百万客户同时发现航班便宜时，该数字可以达到 50,000 TPS。端到端消息往返：大约 100 毫秒。在 20 世纪 90 年代，当其他所有行业都从大型机迁移到 Unix 时，航空公司查看了性能数据并保持原样。替代品无法匹配吞吐量。很多人还是做不到。如今运行 z/TPF 的 IBM Z 系列大型机并不是出于怀旧之情。他们之所以经营这家公司，是因为 60 年来，在这项特定工作上，没有任何其他公司能比它更胜一筹。这有一个教训。我会回来讨论这个问题。 Sabre（该公司）如今还围绕同一领域销售云原生层和 API-first 产品；该行业并行运行旧核心和新表面的频率比头条新闻所暗示的要多。
  5. 我的航班的适用范围 当 Technogise 通过 myBiz 预订我的 ContainerDays 旅行时，该预订触及了该生态系统的特定层面。 MakeMyTrip 使用艾玛迪斯作为其 GDS：该系统诞生于 1987 年法航、汉莎航空、西班牙国家航空和 SAS 之间的合作伙伴关系，现已成为欧洲、印度和亚太大部分地区的主导 GDS。 Amadeus 并未在 1987 年原始 Bull 大型机上运行。它在 20 世纪 90 年代迁移到 Unix，并逐渐转向更现代的架构。但数据模型、协议以及最重要的是代理使用的命令语言（加密模式）仍然与 20 世纪 60 年代的原始设计保持一致。我的 PNR 格式、电子客票的结构、票价的计算方式：所有这些都遵循我出生之前制定的惯例。我的回程航班出现了进一步的复杂情况。出港全部为印度航空（DDTCIV、NAG→DEL→LHR）。印度航空使用 Amadeus Altéa 运营，这是一个建立在 Amadeus 基础设施之上的现代化 PSS（乘客服务系统）。他们于 2023 年迁移到该系统，取代了旧的 SITA 系统，这是亚洲航空史上最大的航空公司 PSS 迁移之一。回程（DHB4AL、MAN→LHR→DEL→NAG）混合英国航空（也由 Amadeus Altéa 运营）和印度航空。由于该航线上的两家航空公司位于同一个 Amadeus 堆栈上，因此单个 PNR 可以跨越两家航空公司，而无需在不相关的 PSS 之间手动构建桥梁。这种一致性使得预订成功。这也是在出现问题时重新安排工作能够顺利进行的原因，而且事情确实出了问题。但我有点超前了。

Original Summary

  1. The Problem That Built an Industry Part 1 of 6 in the Iron Core series: the 60-year-old infrastructure that flies 4.5 billion people a year.
  2. In December 2025, someone at Technogise opened MakeMyTrip's corporate platform, typed in a destination, and booked me two flights to London. The whole thing took under a minute. A confirmation email landed in my inbox. Six-character booking references appeared: DDTCIV and DHB4AL. I was going to speak at ContainerDays 2026. A conference about containers, orchestration, and cloud-native infrastructure: the kind of modern, ephemeral, stateless systems I spend my working life thinking about. The irony only hit me on the flight over. The infrastructure that booked those flights traces its design to the 1960s. It still runs on lineages that predate Unix and speaks command languages built for teletypes. The implementations, hardware, and surrounding software have been replaced and upgraded many times; what persists is the data model, the protocols, and the transaction semantics. None of that happened in a single "big bang" rewrite: it accumulated while the system kept flying, and at peak it still handles on the order of 10,000 transactions per second. I build distributed systems. I thought I understood complex infrastructure. Then I looked at my own boarding pass and pulled the thread. This is a six-part series about what I found.
  3. The World Before SABRE To understand why this infrastructure exists, you need to understand the problem it was built to solve. By the mid-1950s, American Airlines was managing reservations on index cards. A booking required a phone call to an agent, who would search physical card racks across multiple city offices, confirm availability verbally, and call the passenger back. A transatlantic reservation could take 90 minutes to confirm. The airline was processing roughly 85,000 reservation requests a day across 50-plus cities. The system was collapsing. The origin of what would become the GDS (Global Distribution System) is almost mythological. In 1953, C.R. Smith, president of American Airlines, was seated next to R. Blair Smith, an IBM salesman, on a cross-country flight. By the time they landed, the outline of a solution had been sketched. IBM and American Airlines entered a formal development partnership in 1959. The result was SABRE (Semi-Automated Business Research Environment). It went live in 1964. That is six years from handshake to contract, and another five from contract to production: on the order of a decade from conversation to a running reservation system. Big infrastructure has always moved on human calendars, not pitch-deck ones. The same year the IBM System/360 was announced. Three years before the first ATM. Five years before the moon landing. Fifteen years before the first VisiCalc spreadsheet. Within a decade, every major airline followed suit: GDSFoundedOriginal OwnerTech FoundationSABRE1964American Airlines + IBMIBM ACP / TPFApollo1971United AirlinesIBM TPFGalileo1987United + BA + KLM + SwissairIBM TPFWorldspan1990Delta + Northwest + TWAIBM TPFAmadeus1987Air France + Lufthansa + Iberia + SASBull mainframe → Unix Notice the common thread. Four of the five North-American-originated stacks in that table were built on IBM TPF (or its ACP lineage). Amadeus is the exception: Bull, then Unix. They did not all land on the same executable runtime—but they had to solve the same problem shape: huge volume of small, latency-bounded transactions over shared inventory and settlement rules, in an industry where IATA practice and interlining economics made divergence expensive. Which brings me to the piece of technology that most software engineers have never heard of, and which is almost certainly processing a flight booking somewhere in the world as you read this.
  4. TPF: The OS That Refuses to Die Transaction Processing Facility (TPF) is an IBM mainframe operating system descended from ACP, American Airlines' original Airline Control Program. It was designed for one purpose: processing enormous volumes of simple transactions with sub-millisecond response times. It is not Unix. It does not share Unix's lineage, its philosophy, or its abstractions. It predates Unix by a decade. Understanding TPF requires setting aside almost everything you know about modern operating systems: PropertyTPFModern OSProcess modelNo processes. No threads. Short-lived "programs" that execute and exit.Processes, threads, coroutinesMemory modelFixed memory "cells" per transaction. No heap. No dynamic allocation.Virtual memory, heap, GCI/O modelExtremely fast synchronous I/O to DASD (Direct Access Storage)Async I/O, block storage, NVMeSchedulingPreemptive, priority-based, microsecond granularityTypically millisecond granularityFailure modelTransaction-level rollback. The system does not crash; the transaction does.Depends on applicationPrimary languageAssembler. C was added later.Everything TPF is not really an OS in the way you think of one. It is closer to what we would now call a transaction runtime: a system purpose-built to receive a unit of work, execute a short program against it, commit state changes, and immediately move on. The application-facing transaction path is deliberately minimal: no long-lived per-client worker holding connection state in memory between units of work, and no Unix-style thread-per-request model for that path. (System-level scheduling, I/O, and housekeeping still exist—this is not a claim that the machine does nothing between transactions.) This design was made for one workload. It is exceptionally good at that workload. Modern TPF-based systems handle around 10,000 transactions per second under normal conditions. During a fare sale, when millions of customers simultaneously discover that flights are cheap, that number can reach 50,000 TPS. End-to-end message round-trip: roughly 100 milliseconds. In the 1990s, when every other industry was migrating off mainframes to Unix, airlines looked at the performance numbers and stayed put. The replacements couldn't match the throughput. Many still can't. The IBM Z-series mainframes running z/TPF today are not running it out of nostalgia. They are running it because nothing else has beaten it for this specific job in 60 years. There is a lesson in that. I will come back to it. Sabre (the company) today also sells cloud-native layers and API-first products around the same domain; the industry runs old cores and new surfaces in parallel more often than headlines suggest.
  5. Where My Flights Fit Into This When Technogise booked my ContainerDays travel through myBiz, the booking touched a specific layer of this ecosystem. MakeMyTrip uses Amadeus as its GDS: the system born from a 1987 partnership between Air France, Lufthansa, Iberia, and SAS, and now the dominant GDS across Europe, India, and much of Asia-Pacific. Amadeus is not running on the original 1987 Bull mainframe. It migrated to Unix in the 1990s, and has since moved progressively toward a more modern architecture. But the data model, the protocol, and crucially the command language that agents use (cryptic mode) remain continuous with the original 1960s design. The format of my PNR, the structure of my e-ticket, the way the fare is calculated: all of it follows conventions established before I was born. My return flights had a further complication. The outbound was entirely Air India (DDTCIV, NAG→DEL→LHR). Air India runs on Amadeus Altéa, a modern PSS (Passenger Service System) built on top of the Amadeus infrastructure. They migrated to it in 2023, replacing a legacy SITA system in one of the largest airline PSS migrations in Asian aviation history. The return (DHB4AL, MAN→LHR→DEL→NAG) mixed British Airways (who also runs on Amadeus Altéa) and Air India. Because both carriers on that routing sit on the same Amadeus stack, a single PNR could span both airlines without a hand-built bridge between unrelated PSSs. That consistency is what made the booking work. It is also what made the re-accommodation work when things went wrong, and things did go wrong. But I am getting ahead of myself.

评论（默认中文）

arrsingh 2026/04/11 23:34
有趣的是，在文章开头，他们在 1953 年坐在一架飞机上，但 AA 和 IBM 之间直到 1959 年（6 年后）才正式建立合作伙伴关系！这篇文章让这一切看起来神奇地发生得很快，但实际上提醒人们事情需要时间！

>> 几乎是神话。 1953 年，在一次跨国航班上，美国航空公司总裁 C.R. 史密斯 (C.R. Smith) 坐在 IBM 推销员 R. 布莱尔·史密斯 (R. Blair Smith) 旁边。当他们着陆时，解决方案的轮廓已经勾勒出来。 IBM 和美国航空公司于 1959 年建立了正式的开发合作伙伴关系。

编辑：哦，然后实际的系统在 5 年后（1964 年）才真正上线。十多年后，他们两个坐在一起。

当我的潜在客户在我推介后 5 分钟内没有签署交易时，请提醒自己！

Interesting to note right at the start of the article that they sat on a plane next to each other in 1953 but the formal partnership between AA and IBM was not till 1959 - 6 years later! The article makes it look like all this happened magically fast but in reality a reminder that things take time!

>> is almost mythological. In 1953, C.R. Smith, president of American Airlines, was seated next to R. Blair Smith, an IBM salesman, on a cross-country flight. By the time they landed, the outline of a solution had been sketched. IBM and American Airlines entered a formal development partnership in 1959.

edit: oh and then the actual system didn't actually go live another 5 years later - in 1964. Over a decade after the two of them sat next to each other.

Reminder to myself when my potential customers don't sign the deal 5 minutes after my pitch!

在 HN 中查看
uxhacker 2026/04/12 03:37
因此在间歇期IBM和美国航空公司都集中精力研究这个问题。

该系统基于军事消息系统。

值得注意的是，在 SABRE 之前，所使用的系统是随意卖出，直到发出停止消息为止。然后将根据要求进行销售。 如今，这种方法仍在不同的航空公司系统之间使用。

在实施 SABRE 之前，航空公司使用电传打印机作为通信方式。 SABRE 和其他 IBM 360 系统的一些命令直接来自这一时期。例如，AJFkSFO9MAR 是一种节省发送字符的方法。表示 3 月 9 日从肯尼迪机场飞往旧金山的可用座位数。这早于 SABRE。

System 360（像 SABRE 这样的航空公司使用的预订系统）是用 Assembler 编写的有几个原因，而且逻辑与其预订角色紧密相关。例如，它是在打孔卡时代设计的，打孔卡的匹配方法与关系型卡完全不同。 数据库。该逻辑仍然用于将座位与票价相匹配。

就纯粹的速度而言，很大程度上是通过巧妙的工程技巧获得的。乘客记录就是一个例子。这是乘客预订的 9 个字母数字 ID。它是预留的虚拟内存位置的哈希值。 检索它需要 4 个 cpu 周期。

So during the intermittent period IBM and American Airlines were focused on research of the problem.

The system was a based on a military messaging system.

What is important to note is before SABRE the system used was a sell at will until a stop message was issued. Then sales would be on request. This method is still used between different airline systems today.

Before the implementation of SABRE airlines used teleprinters as a way of communicating. Some of the commands SABRE and other IBM 360 systems come directly from this period. For example AJFkSFO9MAR was a way of economizing on characters sent. It means what is the available seats from JFK to San Francisco on the 9th of March. This predates SABRE.

There is several reasons that the System 360 (the reservation systems used by airlines like SABRE) is one that it is written in Assembler, and also the logic is very tied into its role of reservation. For example it was designed in the days of punchcards, which have a totally different method of matching than a relational Database. The logic is still used on matching a seat to a fare.

On the pure speed much of it is gained by clever engineering tricks. An example would be the passenger record. This is 9 alphanumeric id of the passenger reservation. It is the hash of virtual memory location of the reservation. It takes 4 cpu cycles to retrieve it.

rawgabbit 2026/04/12 06:33
SABRE的技术来自SAGE。这是为 NORAD 建造的。

https://www.ibm.com/history/sage

The technology for SABRE came from SAGE. Which was built for NORAD.

https://www.ibm.com/history/sage

在 HN 中查看
WillAdams 2026/04/12 09:44
嗯，有一个老笑话...

三个女人坐在酒吧里讨论她们的恋人，第一个说：“我的恋人是摔跤手，他精力充沛，太棒了！”。

第二个回答道：“我的爱人是一位诗人，他如此浪漫、体贴、敏感，就像童话故事里的一样！”。

第三个人沉默了，另外两个女人满怀期待地看着她，直到她最后叹了口气说：“我的爱人是一名 IBM 推销员，他只是坐在床边告诉我，当我们最终做爱时会有多好。”

Well, there is the old joke...

Three women are sitting in a bar discussing their lovers, the first says, "My lover is a wrestler, he's so energetic, it's _wonderful_!".

The second responds, "My lover is a poet, he's so romantic and thoughtful and sensitive, it's like something out of a fairy tale!".

The third is silent, and the other two women look at her expectantly until she finally sighs and says, "My lover is an IBM salesman, he just sits on the edge of the bed and tells me how good it will be when we eventually make love."

在 HN 中查看
throwup238 2026/04/12 01:44
> 当我的潜在客户在我推介后 5 分钟内没有签署交易时，提醒自己！

典型的企业销售问题是“决策者购买的时间比你保持偿付能力的时间要长”。

> Reminder to myself when my potential customers don't sign the deal 5 minutes after my pitch!

The classic "the decision makers can take longer to buy than you can stay solvent" problem of enterprise sales.

在 HN 中查看
Animats 2026/04/12 14:49
这不是第一个电子预订系统。其前身是 Magnetronic Reservisor，由 Teleregister 为美国航空公司定制。它没有使用通用计算机。它有磁鼓和远程终端，但它不是通用的。

Teleregister 为多家航空公司、铁路和空中交通管制建立了一系列此类专用系统。对于 20 世纪 50 年代初期的这些工作来说，通用 CPU 既太贵又太慢。直到 20 世纪 70 年代中期的小型计算机时代，通用计算机才真正实现了一切。 SABRE 必须等到通用计算机变得更好。

有趣的是，事务处理操作系统已经消亡了。 Tandem 的操作系统就是这样工作的。但是 运行事务程序一次并刷新它的方法几乎已经死了。有趣的是，CGI 程序除外，它们是真正的一次性事务程序，但实现效率低下。

[1] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F4d-OFDs1hY

[2] https://s3data.computerhistory.org/brochures/teleregister.sp...

This wasn't the first electronic reservation system. It was preceded by the Magnetronic Reservisor, custom built for American Airlines by Teleregister. It didn't use general-purpose computers. It had magnetic drums and remote terminals, but it was not general purpose.

Teleregister built a range of such special-purpose systems, for several airlines, railroads, and air traffic control. General purpose CPUs were both too expensive and too slow for these jobs in the early 1950s. Using a general purpose computer for everything didn't really happen until the minicomputer era in the mid-1970s. SABRE had to wait until general-purpose computers got better.

It's interesting that transaction processing operating systems died out. Tandem's OS worked that way. But the run transaction program once and flush it approach is almost dead. Except, amusingly, for CGI programs, which are true use-once transaction programs, with an inefficient implementation.

[1] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F4d-OFDs1hY

[2] https://s3data.computerhistory.org/brochures/teleregister.sp...

在 HN 中查看
StilesCrisis 2026/04/11 23:37
“关键的见解是[...]。没有守护程序。没有后台线程。事务之间的内存中没有持久的连接状态。”

关闭选项卡。

"The key insight is [...]. No daemons. No background threads. No connection state persisted in memory between transactions."

Closed the tab.

在 HN 中查看
croisillon 2026/04/12 00:04
讽刺的是……

  "That is not coincidence — it is the market discovering the optimal solution to a specific problem. When you see that pattern in your own domain, pay attention to it."
ironically...

  "That is not coincidence — it is the market discovering the optimal solution to a specific problem. When you see that pattern in your own domain, pay attention to it."
在 HN 中查看
arrsingh 2026/04/11 23:45
我也注意到了这一点，也翻了个白眼，但我很高兴我继续阅读——这实际上是一篇相当不错的文章。也许作者使用法学硕士来帮助进行一些文案编辑，但可能应该给予它较少的编辑代理权。

不管怎样，我很高兴我读了它并等待该系列的其他部分。我真的很好奇如何访问此航空公司预订数据，以便我可以编写自己的机器人来预订航班并处理所有排列和组合以找到最优惠的价格。

I noticed that too and did roll my eyes as well but I'm glad I kept reading - its actually quite a good article. Maybe the author used an LLM to help do some copy editing but should have probably given it less editorial agency.

Either way I'm glad I read it and waiting for the other parts of the series. Really curious how to get access to this airline booking data so I can write my own bot to book my flights and deal with all the permutations and combinations to find the best deal.

在 HN 中查看
alwa 2026/04/12 02:15
同样在这里——我容忍了语言上的抽搐，我找到了足够的肉来继续前进，但令我困惑的是结论。这感觉像是这种法学硕士写作中智力上危险的部分。例如：

> 趋同进化是真实存在的。每个主要的 GDS 都独立地到达相同的底层平台。这并非巧合——而是市场发现了特定问题的最佳解决方案。

我很难理解 GDS 通过“趋同进化”和市场发现“独立”达到互操作性标准的说法。这不是更接近谢林点，或者网络效应，或者使用“平台”一词来表示“协议”或“标准”吗？

这不是说“HTML是由浏览器的独立趋同演化产生的”吗？喜欢——我 猜测，如果你偏离通用标准，那么你就会失去合作利益——参见 IE6。而我 猜测，一开始就有 Mosaic，Mosaic 讲 HTML，所有后来的人也可能讲 HTML。但这不是趋同进化，而是帮助自己获得标准的合作利益。

当第一批 GDS 在美国诞生时，“市场”受到严格监管。给定地点之间的票价、承运商和班次都是固定的；衬布是一种商业优势；航空公司和旅行社之间的关系明确；等等[0]。 IATA 在全球范围内扩展了标准；你没有 有 如果按照国际航空运输协会的方式来做，那么你就会把生意搁置在桌面上。

如果说有什么不同的话，那就是直接预订 PSS（他提到了 Navitaire [1]）似乎与法学硕士的主张相反。随着市场的开放和业务空间的变化，新的、不同的模型得到了购买，并且出现了新的软件范例来描述它们。直订低成本航空公司世界花了一两年时间（以及全球行业的剧变）才看到与传统 GDS 集成的价值，对吧？

…法学硕士似乎也对标识符识别事物印象深刻：

> 一份 PNR、两家航空公司、相同的基础平台。

> 两张票，两种面额货币，一种底层 NUC 算法将它们联系在一起。

> 一个 9 个字符的字符串位于 PNR 字段中，贯穿四个组织的财务系统。

[0] https://airandspace.si.edu/stories/editorial/airline-deregul...

[1] https://www.phocuswire.com/Jilted-by-JetBlue-for-Sabre-Navit...

Same here—I tolerated the linguistic tics, I found enough meat to keep going, but it’s the conclusions that confuse me. And that feels like the intellectually dangerous part with this sort of LLM writing. For example:

> Convergent evolution is real. Every major GDS independently arrived at the same underlying platform. That is not coincidence — it is the market discovering the optimal solution to a specific problem.

I struggle to understand the claim that GDSes “arrived independently” at interoperability standards through “convergent evolution” and market discovery. Isn’t it something closer to a Schelling point, or a network effect, or using the word “platform” to mean “protocol” or “standard”?

Isn’t it like saying “HTML arose from web browsers’ independent, convergent evolution”? Like—I guess, in that if you diverge from the common standard then you lose the cooperative benefits—see IE6. And I guess, in that in the beginning there was Mosaic, and Mosaic spoke HTML, that all who came after might speak HTML too. But that’s not convergent evolution, that’s helping yourself to the cooperative benefits of a standard.

“The market” was highly regulated when the first GDSes were born in the US. Fares, carriers, and schedules were fixed between given points; interlining was a business advantage; the relationships between airlines and with travel agents were well-defined; and so on [0]. IATA extended standards across the world; you didn’t have to do it the IATA way, but you’d be leaving business on the table.

If anything, it seems like direct-booking PSSes (he mentioned Navitaire [1]) demonstrate the opposite of the LLM’s claim. As the market opened up and the business space changed, new and divergent models found purchase, and new software paradigms emerged to describe them. It took a decade or two (and upheaval in the global industry) before the direct-booking LCC world saw value in integrating with legacy GDSes, right?

…the LLM also seems bizarrely impressed that identifiers identify things:

> One PNR, two airlines, the same underlying platform.

> Two tickets, two currencies of denomination, one underlying NUC arithmetic tying them together.

> One 9-character string, sitting in a PNR field, threading across four organisations' financial systems.

[0] https://airandspace.si.edu/stories/editorial/airline-deregul...

[1] https://www.phocuswire.com/Jilted-by-JetBlue-for-Sabre-Navit...

在 HN 中查看
PaulDavisThe1st 2026/04/12 12:30
> ...法学硕士似乎也对标识符识别事物印象深刻：

[...]

>> 一个 9 个字符的字符串，位于 PNR 字段中， 跨四个组织的财务系统。

（强调已添加）

这不是标识符所识别的。标识符在多个、独立、完全不同的系统中标识相同的事物。

还有其他示例：信用卡号、政府颁发的身份证号。

但总的来说，标识符在生成它们的系统之外几乎没有流通，因此对此有“印象深刻”的元素。

> …the LLM also seems bizarrely impressed that identifiers identify things:

[...]

>> One 9-character string, sitting in a PNR field, threading across four organisations' financial systems.

(emphasis added)

It's not that identifiers identify. It's that an identifier identifies the same thing across multiple, independent, entirely distinct systems.

There are other examples: credit card numbers, government issued ID numbers.

But in general, identifiers have little currency outside the system that generated them, hence the "impressed" element to this.

在 HN 中查看
delusional 2026/04/12 14:43
> 但总的来说，标识符在生成它们的系统之外几乎没有流通

这显然是错误的，因为如果这是真的，我们将无法识别任何东西。标识符仅在某些外部方为标识符指定含义时才有用。两个系统必须选择一个共同的标识符来讨论一个人。他们必须选择一个标识符来讨论技术领域。他们甚至必须选择一个标识符来讨论技术协议。

标识符无处不在。它们通常会被转换为系统边缘的内部内容，但我敢打赌 PNR 也是如此。

> But in general, identifiers have little currency outside the system that generated them

That's clearly wrong, because if it were true we wouldn't be able to identify anything. Identifiers are only useful in so far as some external party assigns a meaning to the identifier. Two systems MUST pick a common idwntifier to discuss a person. They MUST pick an identifier to discuss a technical field. They MUST even pick an identifier to discuss a technical protocol.

Identifiers are everywhere. They'll usually be translated into something internal at the edge of a system, but I bet the PNR is too.

在 HN 中查看
cr125rider 2026/04/11 23:51
你能解释一下为什么这是错误的吗？
Can you explain why that’s wrong?
在 HN 中查看
defen 2026/04/12 00:08
这是 LLM 生成的文本签名。
It's the LLM-generated-text signature.
在 HN 中查看
jim33442 2026/04/12 10:00
这听起来也不是真正的关键见解
That and it also sounds like not really a key insight
在 HN 中查看
iririririr 2026/04/12 02:16 deleted
[死的]
[dead]
在 HN 中查看
vachina 2026/04/12 03:21
整个事情（可能是整个系列）都是 LLM 生成的。兄弟甚至懒得重做生成的流程图。

网站可能是未来产品植入的 SEO 倾斜。

The entire thing (and probably the entire series) is LLM generated. Bro didnt even bother to redo the generated flowchart.

Site is likely SEO slop for future product placement.

在 HN 中查看
kuzivaai 2026/04/12 15:06
实用性胜过时尚架构”——这与我在汽车中看到的东西相符。这些系统可能无法通过所有现代审查，但正确运行的时间比大多数团队成员的生存时间都长。期待 PNR 上的第 2 部分。
Fitness for purpose beats fashionable architecture" — this matches something I've seen in automotive. Systems that would fail every modern review but have been running correctly for longer than most of the team has been alive. Looking forward to Part 2 on the PNR.
在 HN 中查看
conductr 2026/04/12 13:04
有趣的是，他们将 90 分钟的手动预订时间缩短到了微秒。我不确定 Unix 之前的情况到底是什么样子，也许这就是软件的样子，一切都是定制的考验。但是，这让我想知道如果速度不那么快是否会“更好”。比如，更快地构建或更容易维护/改进，我们仍然没有谈论与运行世界的遗留软件的斗争，诸如此类的事情。

即使是 1 秒的交易速度在今天听起来也很慢，但如果它要取代 90 分钟的手动流程，我宁愿现在就有这个解决方案，而不是需要 5-10 年的微秒级快速解决方案。

Interesting they went from 90 minute manual booking time to microseconds. I’m unsure of what the landscape was really like before Unix and such, maybe this was just how software was and everything was a bespoke ordeal. But, it makes me wonder if something less fast could have been “better”. As in, faster to build or easier to maintain/improve, something where we still weren’t talking about wrestling with legacy software that runs the world, those kinds of things.

Even 1 second transaction speed sounds slow today but if it’s replacing a 90 minute manual process I’d rather have that solution now than a microsecond fast solution that takes 5-10 years.

在 HN 中查看
neilv 2026/04/12 00:07
ITA Software与大型机网络集成，并被Google收购。

一位高管公开表示，如果他们不使用 Lisp，他们就不可能做到这一点。

（今天，编程语言的格局在某种程度上更加强大。例如，Rust 获得了 Lisps 提供的一些元编程功能，团队可能已经能够艰难地完成这一任务。）

ITA Software integrated with the mainframe network, and was acquired by Google.

An exec made a public quote that they couldn't have done it if they hadn't used Lisp.

(Today, the programming language landscape is somewhat more powerful. Rust got some metaprogramming features informed by Lisps, for example, and the team might've been able to slog through that.)

在 HN 中查看
zellyn 2026/04/12 01:40
当 Google 搁置他们的 Sabre 替代品时，我感到非常难过。本来可以经营整个行业。
I was so sad when Google shelved their Sabre replacement. Could have run the whole industry.
在 HN 中查看
andai 2026/04/12 02:35
>TPF 并不现代。它无法通过当代工程团队对其应用的所有架构审查。它还每秒处理 50,000 笔交易，硬件延迟低于 100 毫秒，而成本只是同等云占用空间的一小部分。它已经这样做了 60 年。

什么样的审核会失败？听起来它对我来说设计得很好。

>TPF is not modern. It would fail every architectural review a contemporary engineering team would apply to it. It also handles 50,000 transactions per second with sub-100ms latency on hardware that costs a fraction of an equivalent cloud footprint. It has been doing this for 60 years.

What kind of review would it fail? Sounds like it's pretty well designed to me.

在 HN 中查看
jakub_g 2026/04/12 03:55
FWIW，自 2000 年代中期以来，IBM 一直在大幅增加 TPF 成本，这促使 GDS 经过十多年的努力才从 TPF 迁移到云。
FWIW, IBM has been increasing TPF costs dramatically since mid 2000s, which prompted the GDSes to go through decade-plus efforts to migrate away from it, to the cloud.
在 HN 中查看
cr125rider 2026/04/11 23:52
您可以将 RSS 添加到您的网站吗？我很想跟随，但不能。
Can you add RSS to your site? I’d love to follow but can’t.
在 HN 中查看
outside1234 2026/04/11 23:34
思考人工智能如何可能将通用软件的动态改变回这种状态是很有趣的。

在一个免费实施的世界中，我们是否会看到像这样的为目的而构建的系统的回归，在这种系统中，我们定义所需的输入和输出，而人工智能从头开始构建它，完全是出于目的？

It is interesting to think how AI will potentially change the dynamics back to this from general purpose software.

In a world where implementation is free, will we see a return to built for purpose systems like this where we define the inputs and outputs desired and AI builds it from the ground up, completely for purpose?

在 HN 中查看
conductr 2026/04/12 13:15
大概。以 SaaS 为例，这种情况已经发生了。我过去在 HN 上多次提到过这一点，但我的（已成立的）公司一直在推出自己的 CRM 和其他一些人工智能工具。

看来我们可以自己构建一个产品，同时我们还需要与 SaaS 供应商交谈并起草 RFP/需求。我们可以构建它并在需求形成时进行迭代，因此基本上可以只包含我们关心的功能来完成软件，并完全有能力在未来添加功能（SaaS 不承诺的东西），通常在实施开始之前。我们正在搜索所有 SaaS 产品，我预计我们将在 1-2 年内削减 50% 的产品。那些足够复杂或受到监管的系统有一定的保护（如会计系统）。

Probably. It’s already happening with SaaS as an example. I’ve mentioned this on HN a lot in past but my (established) company has been rolling its own CRM and some other tools with AI.

It seems we can build a product ourselves in the same time it would take us to talk to saas vendors and draft the RFP/requirements. We can build it and iterate as the requirements are being forged, so can essentially have completed software with just the features we care about, with full ability to add features in future (something saas doesn’t promise) often before an implementation would even kick off. We’re searching through all our SaaS products and i expect we’ll cut 50% of them in 1-2 years. The ones that are sufficiently complex or regulated have some protection (like accounting systems).

在 HN 中查看
DanielVZ 2026/04/12 00:28
如果没有人工智能，我也是这么想的。还有哪些行业需要尚未提供的低延迟高吞吐量交易？
I was thinking the same sans AI. What other industries require low latency high throughput transactions that haven’t been served yet?
在 HN 中查看
paulnpace 2026/04/11 23:03
> 它...每秒处理 50,000 笔交易，硬件延迟低于 100 毫秒，而成本只是同等云占用空间的一小部分。它已经这样做了 60 年。

吃掉那个，比特币。

> It...handles 50,000 transactions per second with sub-100ms latency on hardware that costs a fraction of an equivalent cloud footprint. It has been doing this for 60 years.

Eat that, Bitcoin.

在 HN 中查看
bombcar 2026/04/11 23:29
每秒 50,000 笔交易对于人类来说已经是一大堆了。

它是 没有什么 即使是古老的 CPU - 更不用说让 Cray 1 哭泣的现代奇迹了。

关键是经过深思熟虑和经过测试的设计。

50,000 transactions a second is a bunch for humans.

It’s nothing for even an ancient CPU - let alone our modern marvels that make a Cray 1 cry.

The key is an extremely well-thought and tested design.

在 HN 中查看
ForOldHack 2026/04/12 02:23
它主要是用 COBOL 编写的。这就是我进入 XENIX/UNIX 的方式。一台机器宕机了，它是一台训练机：它需要 PC 上的 XENIX，因为它在 XENIX 上的 RM/COBOL 上运行，因为所有屏幕和编码都已经用 COBOL 编写。 RM/COBOL 具有古老的兼容性，但代码极其简单，几十年前就已经解决了。 （我是 1985 年拿到的，但所有创建日期都是 1982 年。原始文件肯定是 20 世纪 60 年代中期的。我指出了这一点，有人打电话给我。我找到了 1966 年的培训手册，屏幕是 确切地 相同，当然 3270 状态行除外。

它在 4.77Mhz IBM PC 上速度很快，在 10Mhz V20 上速度更快。

现在，50,000 个事务对于 IBM 大型机来说已经相当标准了？ z/ 系列仍然大致相同，但它可扩展到 32 个处理器。 （对不起，每天数十亿）

It was mostly written in COBOL. This is how I got into XENIX/UNIX. A machine went down that was a training machine: It required XENIX on a PC, because it ran on RM/COBOL on XENIX, because all the screens, and the encoding, already written were in COBOL. RM/COBOL had an ancient compatibility, but the code was extremely simple, having been ironed out many decades earlier. ( I got it in 1985, but all the creation dates were 1982. The original files must have been from the mid 1960s. I pointed this out, and someone called me on it. I found a training manual from 1966, and the screens were exactly The same, except of course for the 3270 status line.

It was fast on an 4.77Mhz IBM PC, and much faster on a 10Mhz V20.

50,000 transactions was pretty standard for a IBM Mainframe, now? The z/ Series is still about the same, but it scales up to 32 processors. ( excuse me, billions. per day )

在 HN 中查看
uxhacker 2026/04/12 03:11
事实上，GDS 使用的大多数系统都是用汇编语言编写的。我知道，因为我曾使用过许多系统。这是 TPF 机器上保留不同系统的原因之一，但不是唯一的原因。
Most of the systems used for GDS’s were in fact written in Assembler. I know as I have worked with a number of the systems. This is one, but not the only reason the different systems have remained on TPF machines.
在 HN 中查看
bombcar 2026/04/12 02:48
还有人认为虚拟化是在 386 上发明的:)
And there are people who think virtualization was invented on the 386 :)
在 HN 中查看
andersmurphy 2026/04/12 01:04
我的意思是，您可以使用 sqlite 和动态语言在 M1 上轻松实现 100K TPS。延迟低于 100 毫秒。

人们不这样做是因为它不时尚（酷孩子都在 AWS 上，拥有数百个容器，托管数千个微服务，因为这是网络规模的）。

I mean you can easily do 100K TPS on a M1 with sqlite and a dynamic language. With sub 100ms latency.

People don't do it because it's not fashionable (the cool kids are all on AWS with hundreds of containers, hosting thousands micro services, because that's web scale).

在 HN 中查看
arter45 2026/04/12 01:42
嗯，这种情况下的事务是业务事务，可能涉及 1 或 N 个远程调用。想象一下对禁飞名单、欺诈检测、航班延误等进行检查。光速也是另一个问题。所以这并不像在本地 SQL 数据库上执行 35k TPS 那么简单。

但是，是的，您并不总是需要很酷的技术。

Well, transactions in this context are business transactions, which may involve 1 or N remote calls. Imagine checks against no fly lists, fraud detection, flight delay and so on. Speed of light is also another concern. So it’s not as simple as doing 35k TPS on a local SQL database.

But yes, you don’t always need cool technologies.

在 HN 中查看
andersmurphy 2026/04/12 03:07
确实如此，但其中很多检查将针对数据的本地快照。

> 但是，是的，您并不总是需要很酷的技术。

具有讽刺意味的是，大型机是非常酷的技术，只是不时尚。他们在指令层面拥有疯狂的一致性保证。热插拔功能等。您很难通过基于现代微服务的云计算的垃圾箱火灾来复制这些功能。

True, but a lot of those checks will be against a local snapshots of the data.

> But yes, you don’t always need cool technologies.

That's kinda the irony mainframes are incredibly cool piece's of tech, just not fashionable. They have insane consistency guarantee at the instruction level. Hot swapping features etc. Features you'd struggle to replicate with the dumpster fire that is modern microservice based cloud computing.

在 HN 中查看
buckle8017 2026/04/11 23:30
啊，是的，谁会想到这是一个完全集中的可扩展系统。

（对于迂腐的人来说，它并不完全是集中式的，也不完全是联合式的，因为每家航空公司都认为自己的世界观是绝对正确的）

Ah yes a completely centralized system that scales, who would have thought.

(For the pedantic, it's not exactly centralized nor federated since each airline treats their view of the world as absolutely correct)

在 HN 中查看
arter45 2026/04/12 01:07
它也不是去中心化的，至少不是比特币意义上的去中心化。参与者之间的互动可能是自动化的，但他们最终可以依赖法律合同和人员。国际航空运输协会 (IATA) 是这些参与者之一，但由于国际航空运输协会 (IATA) 的作用，每个人都必须信任其在航空业中的作用。为避免对中央机构的信任而建立的分散式航空公司系统将有很大不同（实际上，预订部分可能是他们遇到的最小问题）。

它可能不需要所有参与者达成共识（每一步都达成共识就可以了），所以很可能不需要投票。

它甚至不是未经许可的。随机公司不可能仅仅因为可以生成密钥对就加入这个“链”。

这是一个根本不同的问题，架构不同也是有道理的。

It’s not decentralized either, at least not in the Bitcoin sense of the word. Interactions between participants may be automated but they can ultimately rely on legal contracts and people. IATA is one of those participants, but everyone has to trust IATA in the airline industry because of their role. A decentralized airline system built to avoid trust in a central authority would be pretty different (actually the booking part may be the least of their problems there).

It probably doesn’t require consensus among all participants (pairwise consensus at every step should be fine), so there is very likely no voting.

It’s not even permissionless. It’s not like a random company could join this “chain” simply because they can generate a keypair.

It’s a fundamentally different problem, and it makes sense that the architecture is different.

在 HN 中查看
zer00eyz 2026/04/12 00:28
SABRE 提醒我们，设计精良的东西才能发挥作用。

有多少银行和 ERP、有多少会计系统仍在运行 COBOL 脚本？ （很多）。

想想现代网络基础设施以及我们如何部署......

cpu -> 虚拟机管理程序 -> 虚拟机 -> 容器 -> 运行时 -> 库代码 -> 你的代码

我们真的需要堆叠所有这些海龟（抽象）只是为了获取 CPU 的指令吗？

这些层中的每一层都有其他抽象、工具和功能的分支，这些只会增加复杂性和卷积。像 Rust 和 Go 这样的语言编译成可执行文件是一个步骤，下一步可能会重新审视我们如何部署（容器层）……“无服务器”的用例已经存在（和边缘计算），但成本仍然落后，因为软件还没有跟上。

SABRE, is a reminder that things that are well designed just work.

How many banks and ERP's, how many accounting systems are still running COBOL scripts? (A lot).

Think about modern web infrastructure and how we deploy...

cpu -> hypervisor -> vm -> container -> run time -> library code -> your code

Do we really need to stack all these turtles (abstractions) just to get instructions to a CPU?

Every one of those layers has offshoots to other abstractions, tools and functionality that only adds to the complexity and convolution. Languages like Rust and Go compiling down to an executable are a step, revisiting how we deploy (the container layer) is probably on the table next... The use case for "serverless" is there (and edge compute), but the costs are still backwards because the software hasn't caught up yet.

在 HN 中查看
Muromec 2026/04/12 04:40
我曾经参与过一个与 SABRE 和 Amadeus 交互的项目，我不会用“正常工作”来形容它。这个东西也相当缓慢和烦人，因为它的界面是为训练有素的操作员在终端设置中使用它而优化的，而不是为我们这些可怜的家伙通过一些奇怪的 API 螺栓在顶部调用它。

另外，尝试在航空公司网站上检索 PNR 或在航空公司自己的网站上执行类似操作 - 用户体验通常非常糟糕，并且数据加载需要很长时间。 GDS 也难辞其咎。

I used to work on a project that interfaced with both SABRE and Amadeus and "just works" isn't how I would describe it. The thing is also quite slow and annoying, as it's interface is optimized for the trained operator to use it in a terminal setting and not for us poor shcmucks calling it through some weird API bolted on top.

Also, try to retrieve a PNR on an airline website or do like anything on the airline's own website -- the UX is usually pretty bad and the data loading takes forever. For that too the GDS is to blame.

在 HN 中查看
01HNNWZ0MV43FF 2026/04/12 00:54
库代码 - 这是必要的，因为有些事情最好正确完成一次，然后重用。我不会编写自己的日期/时间处理代码。或者加密货币。或者图像编解码器。

运行时间——这使得开发速度更快。 Python、Lua 和 Node.js 项目通常可以比 Rust 和 C++ 重新编译更快地在本地测试小的更改。 （我是作为一个 Rust 专业用户这么说的——链接步骤实在是太慢了。）

容器 - 这为您提供了“apt-get”的虚拟实例。系统包管理器无法更改，因此我们对它们进行抽象并重用工作代码来满足新的需求。我是第二次在 Docker 中构建一些东西，如果我尝试安装依赖项，它会破坏我的主机系统。该软件在 Ubuntu 22.04 上运行得很好，但现在我在 2026 年的 Debian 上运行。这里我重用了有效的代码，对吗？

VM - 容器不是安全沙箱。虚拟机允许多个租户相对安全地共享硬件。当 Spectre 黑客事件出现时，我并没有惊慌 - 云主机在他们的级别上处理了它。如果没有虚拟机，每个人都必须运行自己的专用硬件？我会为我的概念验证应用程序购买专用的 CPU 核心吗？虚拟机相当于电网的软件 - 每个人都共享每个发电站，而不是每个人都过度配置他们可能需要的最大发电机。当传输线掉线时，灯会闪烁并保持常亮。一旦你意识到，当你_几乎_失去动力但又没有失去动力的那半秒的瞬间，你需要付出多少努力，以及从中带来多少便利，你就会感到惊叹不已。

虚拟机管理程序 - 虚拟机管理程序只管理虚拟机，对吧？

快点。不要快活地走到栅栏前。大部分的存在都是有原因的。

Library code - This is necessary because some things are best done correctly, just once, and then reused. I am not going to write my own date/time handling code. Or crypto. Or image codecs.

Run time - This makes development faster. Python, Lua, and Node.js projects can typically test out small changes locally faster than Rust and C++ can recompile. (I say this as a pro Rust user - The link step is so damned slow.)

Container - This gives you a virtual instance of "apt-get". System package managers can't change, so we abstract over them and reuse working code to fit a new need. I am this very second building something in Docker that would trash my host system if I tried to install the dependencies. It's software that worked great on Ubuntu 22.04, but now I'm on Debian from 2026. Here I am reusing code that works, right?

VM - Containers aren't a security sandbox. VMs allow multiple tenants to share hardware with relative safety. I didn't panic when the Spectre hacks came out - The cloud hosts handled it at their level. Without VMs, everyone would have to run their own dedicated hardware? Would I be buying a dedicated CPU core for my proof-of-concept app? VMs are the software equivalent of the electrical grid - Instead of everyone over-provisioning with the biggest generator they might ever need, everyone shares every power station. When a transmission line drops, the lights flicker and stay on. It's awe-inspiring once you realize how much work goes into, and how much convenience comes out of, that half-second blip when you _almost_ lose power but don't.

Hypervisor - A hypervisor just manages the VMs, right?

Come on. Don't walk gaily up to fences. Most of it's here for a reason.

在 HN 中查看
zer00eyz 2026/04/12 02:10
> 大部分内容的存在都是有原因的。

您对主机操作系统、虚拟操作系统、容器的争论正是我的重点。我们没有解决安全性和可安装性问题，而是构建了更多的工具、更多的抽象层。每个都有开销、安全面和复杂性。

与其解决 Rust 性能（在构建时），不如改用一种速度更快但开销更大、安全性更高、复杂性更高的语言。

你已经分解了我们为了避免在底层解决问题而构建的海龟堆栈......

本文所讨论的 SABRE 与此截然相反，它向我们暗示，更多的抽象层并不总是解决方案的途径。

> Most of it's here for a reason.

Your argument for host os, virtual os, container is the very point im making. Rather than solve for security and installablity, we built more tooling, more layers of abstraction. Each have overhead, security surface and complexity.

Rather than solve Rusts performance (at build time), switch to a language that is faster but has more overhead, more security surface, more complexity.

You have broken down the stack of turtles that we have built to avoid solving the problem, at the base level...

SABRE, what the article is discussing, is the polar opposite of this, it gives us a hint that more layers of abstraction arent always the path to solutions.

在 HN 中查看
noAnswer 2026/04/12 04:07
如果你稍微戳一下，你最终会得到 Java 异常。因为AI文章在撒谎。它不是在未更改的裸机上运行的 60 年前的代码。随着时间的推移，事情被重新实施。
If you poke it a little you will eventually get Java exceptions. Because the AI article is lying. It is not 60 year old code running on unchanged bare metal. Things got reimplemented over time.
在 HN 中查看
PaulDavisThe1st 2026/04/12 12:35
TFA 从未在任何地方声称 SABRE 或 Amadeus 或其他类似系统正在使用 60 年前的代码。
Nowhere does TFA claim that SABRE or Amadeus or other similar systems are using 60 year old code.
在 HN 中查看
ForOldHack 2026/04/12 02:26
据我所知，有一些商店运行 GE 大型机的 java 模拟器，运行... Multics。有人告诉我这件事，我很震惊。多用途。
There are shops, I know of that run a java emulator of a GE Mainframe running... Multics. Someone told me that, and I was floored. Multics.
在 HN 中查看
dijksterhuis 2026/04/12 07:50
虽然我们可以从过去吸取教训，但我们可能不应该带着玫瑰色的太阳镜来看待它；）

---

sabre，拥有并建造 当前的 的版本 系统 大公司使用SABRE 今天，使用父母和您提到的所有这些东西

> 可扩展且安全的 Google Cloud 原生基础架构。支持模块化的微服务架构。 API - 开放平台的第一种方法。 [0]

> 我们从头开始重建 Sabre：云原生技术、人工智能融入基础，心中只有一个目标。你的成功。 [1]

是的……现在是“人工智能驱动”了。

[0]： https://www.sabre.com/resources/viewpoints/offer-order-strat... （跳至“设计不同”标题）

[1]： https://www.sabre.com/about/

---

> 我们真的需要堆叠所有这些海龟（抽象）只是为了获取 CPU 的指令吗？

不。但这些抽象是针对诸如扩展、可靠性、冗余、灵活性等事物，以及一堆与仅向 CPU 获取一些指令无关的其他事物。海龟的数量有所增加，因为与 20 世纪 60 年代相比，如今客户对软件的要求更高。

有时我们需要具有最少依赖性的最简单的解决方案。有时我们需要很多海龟......这实际上取决于我们面前的问题。

while we can learn from the past, we probably shouldn't look at it through sunglasses that are rose-tinted ;)

---

sabre, the company that owns and builds the current version of the system SABRE used by major companies today, uses all of those things the parent and you mentioned

> Google Cloud-native infrastructure that is scalable and secure. Microservice-enabled architecture that supports modularity. API-first approach for an open platform. [0]

> We rebuilt Sabre from the ground up: cloud-native technology, AI baked into the foundation, one goal in mind. Your success. [1]

yeah ... it's 'ai powered' now.

[0]: https://www.sabre.com/resources/viewpoints/offer-order-strat... (skip to the 'different by design' heading)

[1]: https://www.sabre.com/about/

---

> Do we really need to stack all these turtles (abstractions) just to get instructions to a CPU?

no. but those abstractions are there for things like scaling, reliability, redundancy, flexibility, ... and a bunch of other things not related to solely getting some instructions to a CPU. the number of turtles has increased because customers have more requirements for software today than they used to have in the 1960s.

sometimes we need the simplest solution with fewest dependencies. sometimes we need lots of turtles... it really depends on the problem in front of us.

在 HN 中查看
